title: Hybrid alienation: reproductive labour and emancipation in the anthropocene
author: Pierer

opening_move.description: The paper opens by identifying a gap in accounts of alienation: standard Marxist alienation theory neglects reproductive labour and reifies 'nature', while attempts to address non-human agency in political theory lack the critical vocabulary to link nature to questions of power and emancipation.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A 'hybrid' account of alienation that centres reproductive labour can critique arrangements that naturalise reproduction without falling back on a reified notion of nature — connecting labour to emancipation in the Anthropocene

rivals_engaged: political ecology accounts of non-human agency
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper synthesises Marxist alienation critique with feminist political economy and political ecology to produce a 'hybrid' functionalist account that takes seriously both human and non-human labour in constituting the collective.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: conceptual reconstruction; functionalist analysis of labour and reproduction; feminist and Marxist theory
epistemic_mode.notes: Full-length research article (19 pages). Entirely conceptual-normative; no empirical data. The argument moves between feminist political economy, Marxist theory, and political ecology.

central: Wages for Housework movement (Dalla Costa, James)

confidence_notes: Full-length research article. Fully normative despite Anthropocene/ecological subject matter. No empirical evidence. The synthesis of hybrid labour + alienation is the original theoretical contribution.
